questions.text: Which enthalpy change is relevant in the following processNa(s) -> Na (g) ΔH = +107
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Option A, enthalpy of atomization, is the energy required to completely separate all the atoms in a chemical substance. The separated atoms are in a gaseous state and get separated from the corresponding substance in its standard state. Sodium is solid in its standard state, and after atomization, the atoms are in their gaseous state. Hence, this is the correct answer. Option B is the enthalpy change when a solid substance melts into a liquid. Here, the state change is from a solid to a gas, no liquid state involved.Option C is the enthalpy change when a liquid is vaporized into a gas. Here, a solid is atomized to its gaseous state so, vaporization does not apply here.Option D is the enthalpy change when one mole of a substance is formed from its constituent elements. This does not describe the given reaction.

questions.text: Which one is not related to evaporation?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This term refers to a process that happens without interruption. Evaporation can be continuous, but it is not a defining characteristic of the process itself.
Evaporation is indeed an endothermic process, meaning it absorbs heat from the surroundings. This is a key feature of evaporation.
This term describes a process that releases heat. Since evaporation absorbs heat, it is not exothermic, making it the correct answer to the question.

This term means that a process can occur on its own without external influence. Evaporation can be spontaneous under certain conditions, but it is not inherently linked to the definition of evaporation.

questions.explanation: Evaporation is a continuous and spontaneous process. It requires absorption of heat from surroundings, so it is endothermic. Therefore, exothermic is not related to evaporation

questions.text: The enthalpy change of the reaction H2(g) + ½ O2 -> H2O (l) under standard conditions is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Enthalpy change of formation of water
options.explanation: This describes the enthalpy when 1 mole of water is formed from its elements under standard conditions.
This applies to acid-base reactions, not the formation of water from hydrogen and oxygen.

options.text: Both; Enthalpy change of combustion of Hydrogen and enthalpy change of formation of water
options.explanation: The reaction involves the combustion of hydrogen to form water, fulfilling both definitions under standard conditions.

options.text: Enthalpy change of combustion of Hydrogen
options.explanation: This describes the enthalpy change when hydrogen combusts to form water, but it does not include the context of formation.

questions.explanation: Since 1 mole of H2 is being combusted, it can be called enthalpy change of combustion of H2. Also, 1 mole of water is being formed from its constituent elements under standard state so it can also be called standard enthalpy change of formation of water.

questions.text: Which one of the following enthalpy changes is always exothermic?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Combustion is an oxidation reaction that produces heat, and it is therefore always exothermic. Since these reactions are exothermic so the value for the enthalpy change (AH) is always negative

The enthalpy of formation is the standard reaction enthalpy for the formation of the compound from its elements (atoms or molecules) in their most stable reference states at the chosen temperature (298.15K).It may be both exothermic & endothermic.
Enthalpy of atomization is the change in enthalpy when one mole of bonds is completely broken to obtain atoms in the gas phase. For example: atomization of methane molecule.It is always endothermic.
Enthalpies of solution may be either positive or negative - in other words, some ionic substances dissolve endothermically (for example, NaCl); others dissolve exothermically (for example NaOH).

questions.explanation: Combustion is an oxidation reaction that produces heat, and it is therefore always exothermic. Since these reactions are exothermic so the value for the enthalpy change (ΔH) is always negative

questions.text: Thermochemistry is very important to learn about _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: AlI of these options are correct

questions.explanation: This question has been repeated in multiple other papers, hence indicating that this is a high-yield concept. Thermochemistry is the part of thermodynamics that studies the relationship between heat and chemical reactions.

questions.text: The change in heat energy in a chemical reaction at constant temperature and pressure is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Enthalpy is a thermodynamic quantity that represents the total heat content of a system at constant pressure. The enthalpy change in a chemical reaction, often denoted as ΔH, is the heat absorbed or released during the reaction at constant pressure. It includes both the internal energy change and the work done on or by the system.

The heat of sublimation is the energy required or released when a substance changes directly from a solid to a gas without passing through the liquid phase. This process involves both breaking intermolecular forces in the solid and overcoming the energy barrier to convert the substance into a gas.

Internal energy (U) is the sum of the kinetic and potential energies of the particles within a system. The internal energy change in a chemical reaction is denoted as ΔU. ΔU is related to the heat (q) and work (w) done in a system through the first law of thermodynamics: ΔU = q - w. At constant volume, ΔU is equal to the heat change (q) because no work is done.

The heat of formation (ΔHf) is the enthalpy change when one mole of a substance is formed from its elements in their standard states. It is often used to calculate the enthalpy change for a reaction by subtracting the sum of the heats of formation of reactants from the sum of the heats of formation of products: ΔH = ΣΔHf(products) - ΣΔHf(reactants). The standard state refers to the most stable form of an element at a specified temperature and pressure.

questions.explanation: The change in heat energy in a chemical reaction at constant temperature and pressure is called enthalpy change. The enthalpy of sublimation, or heat of sublimation, is the heat required to change one mole of a substance from solid-state to a gaseous state at a given combination of temperature and pressure. The change in the internal energy of a system is the sum of the heat transferred and the work done. Heat of formation of a compound is the change of enthalpy during the formation of 1 mole of the substance from its constituent elements

questions.text: The enthalpy change accompanying the gain of an electron by a neutral gaseous atom to form a negative ion is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is wrong as the ionization potential, in chemistry, is the amount of energy required to remove an electron from an isolated atom or molecule.
Option B is wrong as the electronegativity is a measure of the tendency of an atom to attract a shared pair of electrons.
When an electron is added to a neutral gaseous atom to convert it into a negative ion, the enthalpy change accompanying the process is called Electron affinity enthalpy. Hence, option C is correct.
Option D is wrong as the lattice energy is defined as the energy required to separate a mole of an ionic solid into gaseous ions.
Option E is wrong as the potential energy is energy that is stored or conserved in an object or a substance.

questions.explanation: When an electron is added to a neutral gaseous atom to convert it into a negative ion, the enthalpy change accompanying the process is called Electron affinity enthalpy. Ionization potential, in chemistry, is the amount of energy required to remove an electron from an isolated atom or molecule. Electronegativity is a measure of the tendency of an atom to attract a bonding pair of electrons. Lattice energy is defined as the energy required to separate a mole of an ionic solid into gaseous ions. Potential energy is energy that is stored – or conserved - in an object or substance.

questions.text: The heat of a reaction can be calculated by using:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is wrong as Joule's law is concerned with the heat produced during the passage of current through a resistance in a specific time i.e. H=I²Rt

Option B is wrong as Ohm's law is concerned with the relation of current with voltage. It states that current is directly proportional to the potential difference provided the resistance remains constant.

Hess' law states that the change of enthalpy in a chemical reaction (i.e. the heat of reaction at constant pressure) is independent of the pathway between the initial and final states of the reaction. If a chemical change takes place by several different routes, the overall enthalpy change is the same, i.e. the sum of all of the enthalpies of different routes (provided the initial and final conditions are the same). Hess's law allows the enthalpy change (delta H) for a reaction to be calculated even when it cannot be measured directly.
Faradays law states that emf of an cell is related to the N times the rate of change of electric flux.
Boyle's law states that pressure of a gas is inversely related to volume if the temperature is kept constant.

questions.explanation: Hess' law states that the change of enthalpy in a chemical reaction (i.e. the heat of reaction at constant pressure) is independent of the pathway between the initial and final states. In other words, if a chemical change takes place by several different routes, the overall enthalpy change is the same, regardless of the route by which the chemical change occurs (provided the initial and final conditions are the same). Hess’s law allows the enthalpy change (ΔH) for a reaction to be calculated even when it cannot be measured directly.

questions.text: In an adiabatic process:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Pressure is not maintained constant in an adiabatic process.
The gas is not isothermally expanded in an adiabatic process.

The adiabatic process is defined as a process in which no heat transfer takes place. This does not mean that the temperature is constant, but rather that no heat is transferred into or out of the system.

options.text: System exchanges heat with the surroundings
options.explanation: The system does not exchange heat with the surroundings because the heat exchange is zero.

questions.explanation: During an adiabatic process, the system is completely insulated from its surroundings. Thus, it takes place without heat entering or leaving the system, i.e.,q=0. During an isobaric process, the pressure of the system is kept constant (ΔP=0). During the isochoric process, the volume of the system is kept constant. (ΔV=0). During an isothermal process. the temperature of the system is kept constant (ΔT=0 but q≠0).

questions.text: Cl + e ----> Cl-ΔH = -348kJ/molThe value -348KJ/mol in this case will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ionization energy is the energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom. Here, an electron is being added, not removed.
Electronegativity refers to an atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a bond. This process does not involve forming a bond, but rather adding an electron to form an ion.
Electron affinity is the energy released when an electron is added to a neutral atom to form a negative ion, which is exactly what occurs with Cl in this reaction.
Entropy measures the disorder or randomness in a system. It does not directly relate to the energy change associated with adding an electron to an atom.
Gibbs free energy measures the maximum reversible work done by a thermodynamic system at constant temperature and pressure. It is not specifically about adding an electron to an atom.

questions.explanation: As it can be seen in the equation, electrons are gained by the element chlorine to form a negative ion (anion). The process in which electrons are gained is called electron affinity.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is NOT true for the first law of thermodynamics?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Total energy of the system and surroundings is conserved
options.explanation: This statement is true. The first law of thermodynamics, also known as the law of conservation of energy, states that the total energy within an isolated system, encompassing both the system and its surroundings, remains constant. Energy can change forms or be transferred, but the total amount remains conserved.

options.text: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed
options.explanation: This statement is true and is a fundamental aspect of the first law of thermodynamics. Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed from one form to another, maintaining the overall energy balance within the system.

options.text: The first law is synonymous with the law of conservation of energy
options.explanation: This statement is true. The first law of thermodynamics is essentially the same as the law of conservation of energy, emphasizing that energy is conserved in any physical or chemical process.

options.text: Total energy of the system is increasing
options.explanation: This statement is NOT true. According to the first law of thermodynamics, the total energy of an isolated system remains constant. While energy can be transformed or transferred, the total energy does not increase or decrease, making this statement incorrect.

questions.explanation: The first law of thermodynamics, also known as the law of conservation of energy, asserts that the total energy within an isolated system is constant. Energy may be transformed from one form to another or transferred between the system and its surroundings, but it cannot be created or destroyed. Therefore, the statement in Option D is incorrect as it contradicts this fundamental principle by suggesting that the total energy of the system is increasing.Options A, B, and C correctly reflect the principles of the first law of thermodynamics, emphasizing the conservation of energy and its equivalence to the law of conservation of energy.

questions.text: The reaction 2CO + O2 -> 2CO2 proceeds slower because activation energy is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Activation energy is not related to equilibrium. Equilibrium refers to the state where the rate of the forward reaction is equal to the rate of the reverse reaction, and it doesn't provide information about the speed of the reaction or the activation energy

Activation energy is not a constant for a particular reaction. It is a specific value for that reaction under given conditions. However, it can be affected by changes in temperature and other factors.

If the activation energy is low, it means that the reactants require relatively less energy to overcome the energy barrier and transform into products. In this case, the reaction is likely to occur more quickly because a larger fraction of the molecules will have sufficient energy to react.

The activation energy of a chemical reaction is closely related to its rate. Specifically, the higher the activation energy, the slower the chemical reaction will be. This is because molecules can only complete the reaction once they have reached the top of the activation energy barrier.

questions.explanation: The activation energy of a chemical reaction is closely related to its rate. Specifically, the higher the activation energy, the slower the chemical reaction will be. This is because molecules can only complete the reaction once they have reached the top of the activation energy barrier.

questions.text: One Calorie is equal to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 1 kilo calorie is equal to 4.18 kilojoules (kJ).This is the conversion factor commonly used in the context of nutrition and the energy content of food. It is also known as a kilocalorie (kcal).
1 calorie is equal to 4.18 joules (J).This is the smaller calorie and is often used in scientific contexts, especially in the field of chemistry and physics.
This is not a valid conversion for a calorie. It looks like a molar quantity, but it doesn't correspond to the energy content of one calorie.
This is not the correct conversion for a calorie. The correct conversion is 4.18 kJ.

questions.explanation: 1 calorie equals 4.18 J. It is very often confused with 1 kcal, which equals to 4.18 kJ

questions.text: For a reaction given below C(s) + O2 (g) -> CO2 (g) -394 kJ / molThe enthalpy of reaction is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the positive sign would indicate an endothermic reaction, but the reaction given is exothermic.
This option is correct. The negative sign indicates that the reaction is exothermic, and the magnitude (394 kJ/mol) represents the amount of heat released per mole of reactants.
This option is incorrect. Not only is the sign incorrect (indicating an endothermic reaction), but the magnitude is also incorrect. The reaction releases 394 kJ/mol.
This option is incorrect. While the sign correctly indicates an exothermic reaction, the magnitude is incorrect. The reaction releases 394 kJ/mol, not 197 kJ/mol.

questions.explanation: The formation of carbon dioxide from carbon and oxygen is an exothermic reaction, meaning it releases heat. The enthalpy change, denoted as ΔH, is negative for exothermic reactions. In this case, the reaction releases 394 kJ/mol of energy, so the correct enthalpy change is -394 kJ/mol. The negative sign is crucial as it indicates the direction of heat flow (from the system to the surroundings). Option B correctly reflects this with -394 kJ/mol. The other options are incorrect either due to the wrong sign or incorrect magnitude.

questions.text: Molar heat of vaporization is the amount of heat required to convert one mole of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: A liquid into its vapours at its boiling point
options.explanation: The heat of vaporization is defined as the amount of heat needed to turn 1g of a liquid into a vapor, without a rise in the temperature of the liquid i.e. at a constant temperature which is the boiling point. At B.P the temperature of a liquid remains the same. Hence, this is the correct option.
This option is also correct but here the limitation of temperature cannot be applied. Hence, we may consider it as incorrect.
This option is incorrect as the conversion of solid into vapors (i.e. liquid) is not vaporization. Rather it is called melting.

options.text: Solid into liquid at its melting point
options.explanation: Solid to liquid at its melting point is called melting not vaporization. Hence, this option is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: Molar heat of vapourization refers to the conversion of one mole of a liquid to its vapors at a constant temperature, which is the boiling point of the liquid. That is why option A is correct.Option B is partially correct because vaporization refers to the conversion of a liquid to gas, however, at a constant temperature. Options C and D are incorrect because vaporization does not involve the conversion of solids to liquids.

questions.text: Starting point of Kelvin scale is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The Kelvin scale is an absolute scale given by Lord Kelvin. The starting point for this scale is marked as zero kelvin i.e. -273.15 Degrees Celsius. Hence, this is the correct option.
-400K is not the starting temperature because this scale starts from the absolute zero point.
This is also incorrect as -210k is not the starting temperature because this scale starts from the absolute zero point.
-273.15k is 0 degree Celsius. Hence, this option is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: A change of one kelvin is the same amount of temperature change as one degree Celsius, but the Kelvin scale is “absolute” in the sense that it starts at absolute zero, or what Kelvin and other scientists called “infinite cold.” (0 K = -273.15 degrees C = -459.67 degrees F.)

questions.text: When two moles of H2 and one mole of O2 react to form H2O, -484 KJ heat is evolved. What is Δ Hf for one mole of H2O?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Firstly, since heat is evolved, the formation of water is an exothermic reaction and so the sign of enthalpy change will be negative. Secondly, enthalpy of formation is the change in enthalpy when one mole of a substance is formed from its constituent elements under standard conditions.When 2 moles of H2 and 1 mole of O2 react, 2 moles of H2O will be formed:2H2 + O2 —------------------------> 2H2O ΔH=-484 kJ/mol (given)Now, for the formation of one mole of H2O, we can simply divide the entire equation by 2:H2 + ½ O2 —-----------------------> H20 ΔH= -242 kJ/molSo, the enthalpy change of formation is –242 kJ/mol, which corresponds to option B, hence that is the correct answer.

Firstly, since heat is evolved, the formation of water is an exothermic reaction and so the sign of enthalpy change will be negative. Secondly, enthalpy of formation is the change in enthalpy when one mole of a substance is formed from its constituent elements under standard conditions.When 2 moles of H2 and 1 mole of O2 react, 2 moles of H2O will be formed:2H2 + O2 —------------------------> 2H2O ΔH=-484 kJ/mol (given)Now, for the formation of one mole of H2O, we can simply divide the entire equation by 2:H2 + ½ O2 —-----------------------> H20 ΔH= -242 kJ/molSo, the enthalpy change of formation is –242 kJ/mol, which corresponds to option B, hence that is the correct answer.

Firstly, since heat is evolved, the formation of water is an exothermic reaction and so the sign of enthalpy change will be negative. Secondly, enthalpy of formation is the change in enthalpy when one mole of a substance is formed from its constituent elements under standard conditions.When 2 moles of H2 and 1 mole of O2 react, 2 moles of H2O will be formed:2H2 + O2 —------------------------> 2H2O ΔH=-484 kJ/mol (given)Now, for the formation of one mole of H2O, we can simply divide the entire equation by 2:H2 + ½ O2 —-----------------------> H20 ΔH= -242 kJ/molSo, the enthalpy change of formation is –242 kJ/mol, which corresponds to option B, hence that is the correct answer.

Firstly, since heat is evolved, the formation of water is an exothermic reaction and so the sign of enthalpy change will be negative. Secondly, enthalpy of formation is the change in enthalpy when one mole of a substance is formed from its constituent elements under standard conditions.When 2 moles of H2 and 1 mole of O2 react, 2 moles of H2O will be formed:2H2 + O2 —------------------------> 2H2O ΔH=-484 kJ/mol (given)Now, for the formation of one mole of H2O, we can simply divide the entire equation by 2:H2 + ½ O2 —-----------------------> H20 ΔH= -242 kJ/molSo, the enthalpy change of formation is –242 kJ/mol, which corresponds to option B, hence that is the correct answer.

questions.explanation: To determine the enthalpy change of formation (ΔHf) for one mole of H2O, we can use the given information about the heat evolved in the reaction.The balanced equation for the reaction is:2H2(g) + O2(g) → 2H2O(g)According to the given information, when two moles of H2 and one mole of O2 react, 484 kJ of heat is evolved. This heat change represents the enthalpy change of the reaction.Since two moles of H2O are formed in the reaction, we need to find the enthalpy change for one mole of H2O. To do that, we divide the heat evolved by the stoichiometric coefficient of H2O:ΔHf (H2O) = (484 kJ) / 2ΔHf (H2O) = 242 kJTherefore, the enthalpy change of formation for one mole of H2O is 242 kJ.Firstly, since heat is evolved, the formation of water is an exothermic reaction and so the sign of enthalpy change will be negative. Secondly, enthalpy of formation is the change in enthalpy when one mole of a substance is formed from its constituent elements under standard conditions.When 2 moles of H2 and 1 mole of O2 react, 2 moles of H2O will be formed:2H2 + O2 —------------------------> 2H2O ΔH=-484 kJ/mol (given)Now, for the formation of one mole of H2O, we can simply divide the entire equation by 2:H2 + ½ O2 —-----------------------> H20 ΔH= -242 kJ/molSo, the enthalpy change of formation is –242 kJ/mol, which corresponds to option B, hence that is the correct answer.

questions.text: Which one of the following equations correctly defines the enthalpy changes of formation of carbon monoxide:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: "The standard enthalpy of formation or standard heat of formation of a compound is the change of enthalpy during the formation of 1 mole of the substance from its constituent elements in their reference state, with all substances in their standard states" Since we are talking about the enthalpy of formation, we must ensure that the product has only one mole and the reactants are in their standard forms. The answer is A since carbon naturally exists as a solid whereas Oxygen gas exists as diatomic molecules. Moreover, stoichiometry must also be considered in which equal moles of carbon and oxygen are present on both, the product and the reactant side.
Carbon exists in solid , in this option it is being gas which is wrong.
Oxygen exists as diatomic molecule. In this option it is single atom which is wrong.
This reaction as a whole is wrong , for formation we take elements not compounds as in this option CO is being formed from CO2.

questions.explanation: Since we are talking about the enthalpy of formation, we must ensure that the product has only one mole and the reactants are in their elemental forms. The answer is A since carbon naturally exists as a solid whereas Oxygen gas exists as diatomic molecules. Moreover, stoichiometry must also be considered in which equal moles of carbon and oxygen are present on both, the product and the reactant side.

questions.text: The net heat change in a chemical reaction is the same whether it is brought about in two or more different ways in one or several steps. This is known as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Henry's law relates to the solubility of gases in liquids and the pressure above the liquid, not to energy changes in reactions.

Hess's Law states that the total enthalpy change of a chemical reaction is the same, regardless of the number of steps or the specific pathway taken. It relies on the concept of enthalpy being a state function.
Joule's principle deals with the conversion of electrical energy into heat, specifically in resistive materials, and is not related to chemical reaction pathways.
Dalton's law pertains to the pressures of gases in a mixture, not to the enthalpy changes of chemical reactions.

questions.explanation: Hess's Law is a fundamental principle in thermochemistry, asserting that the total enthalpy change during a chemical reaction is the same regardless of the number of steps in which the reaction is carried out, because enthalpy is a state function. This means that it depends only on the initial and final states of the system, not on the path taken.Henry's law, Dalton's law, and Joule's principle all deal with different principles: Henry's law is about gas solubility in liquids, Dalton's law is about partial pressures in gas mixtures, and Joule's principle is about the conversion of electrical energy to heat in conductors. None of these involve the calculation of enthalpy changes in chemical reactions.

questions.text: A catalyst increases the rate of reaction by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because while raising the temperature can increase reaction rates, it is not a function of a catalyst. Catalysts specifically lower the activation energy without changing temperature.

This option is incorrect as well; while increasing reactant concentration can speed up a reaction, it does not describe the role of a catalyst, which alters the reaction pathway rather than the concentration of reactants.

options.text: Providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
options.explanation: This is the correct option as it accurately describes the primary function of a catalyst. By offering a different pathway, the catalyst decreases the activation energy, allowing more molecules to participate in the reaction.

options.text: Shifting the equilibrium towards the products.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because while catalysts can speed up the rate at which equilibrium is reached, they do not change the position of the equilibrium itself.

questions.explanation: A catalyst speeds up a reaction without being consumed by providing a different mechanism or pathway for the reaction to follow. This alternative pathway has a lower activation energy, which allows a greater fraction of reactant molecules to react per unit of time.

questions.text: Work done on the system is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Work done on system in chem is positive and in phy is negative.
Work done on system in chem is positive and in phy is negative.
Work done on system in chem is positive and in phy is negative.
Work done is phy is positive and in chem is negative.

questions.explanation: Work done on the system is positive in chemistry and negative in physics.Similarly work done by the system is negative in chemistry and positive in physics.

questions.text: If pressure and volume of a gas are variable while temperature remains constant, this belongs to
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This law describes the relationship between the volume and temperature of a gas, assuming pressure is constant. It states that the volume of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.Mathematically:

Avogadro's Law deals with the relationship between the volume of a gas and the number of moles of gas, assuming pressure and temperature are constant. It states that equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain an equal number of molecules.Mathematically:
Avogadro's Law: V∝n (Volume and amount of gas at constant temperature and pressure).

Pascal's Law is related to fluid mechanics and doesn't specifically apply to gases. It states that a change in pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished to all portions of the fluid and to the walls of its container.

questions.explanation: Boyle's law is a gas law, stating that the pressure and volume of a gas have an inverse relationship. If volume increases, then pressure decreases and vice versa, when the temperature is held constant.

questions.text: If a graph is plotted between pressure on x- axis and volume on y- axis for Boyle's law verification, the curve obtained is called
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This term is not commonly used in the context of gas laws. It doesn't represent any specific concept related to Boyle's Law or the graphical representation of pressure and volume.

An isotherm is a curve representing the relationship between two variables (in this case, pressure and volume) at constant temperature. For Boyle's Law, the temperature is held constant, and as a result, the graph obtained is called an isotherm. The equation for Boyle's Law is PV=constant at constant temperature.
The term "biotherm" is not related to Boyle's Law or the graphical representation of pressure and volume. It does not have significance in the context of gas laws.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: If a graph is outlined in between pressure on the x-axis (abscissa) and volume on the y-axis (ordinate), then a curve is obtained. This curve is called isotherm 'iso' implies the same, “therm” implies heat.

questions.text: In exothermic reactions, heat is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. In exothermic reactions, heat is released or given out to the surroundings, not taken in. Exothermic reactions typically result in an increase in temperature of the surroundings.
This statement is correct. Exothermic reactions release energy in the form of heat to the surroundings. Examples of exothermic reactions include combustion reactions, such as burning wood or fuel.
This statement is incorrect. In exothermic reactions, heat is definitely given out to the surroundings. The term "exothermic" itself implies the release of energy.
This statement is also incorrect, as the correct statement is present in option 2. Exothermic reactions release heat to the surroundings.

questions.explanation: An exothermic reaction is a chemical or physical reaction that releases heat. It gives out energy to its surroundings. The energy needed for the reaction to occur is less than the total energy released.

questions.text: Anything under test or observation in laboratory is called
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. In the context of a laboratory setting, the term "surroundings" typically refers to everything outside the system being studied or observed.

This statement is correct. In scientific terms, a system is the specific part of the universe under consideration. It could be a chemical reaction, a biological process, or any other object or process being studied in the laboratory.

This statement is not typically used in the context of scientific experiments. The term "confined space" is often associated with safety regulations and refers to enclosed spaces with limited access and ventilation.
This statement is incorrect. The correct term for the object or process being studied in a laboratory is often referred to as the "system."

questions.explanation: A system is the set of substances and energy that is being studied. If, for example, reactions are occurring in a jar, everything inside the jar is the system, and everything outside the jar is the surroundings. The surroundings are everything not in the system, which means the rest of the universe.

questions.text: The given diagram shows the enthalpy changes during a chemical reaction. What type of reaction is this?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A describes an endothermic reaction which would have a diagram depicting products, at a higher energy level than the reactants.

This diagram shows a reaction in which the reactants are at a greater energy level than the products, indicating that energy is released during the reaction, i.e. change in enthalpy or (H2-H1) would be negative, indicating that it is an exothermic reaction so, option B is correct.
Option C indicates a process in which the heat energy of the system does not change, but the diagram itself shows a change in enthalpy.
Option D cannot be determined from a mere energy diagram as shown.

questions.explanation: This diagram shows a reaction in which the reactants are at a greater energy level than the products, indicating that energy is released during the reaction, i.e. change in enthalpy or (H2-H1) would be negative, indicating that it is an exothermic reaction so, option B is correct.Option A describes an endothermic reaction which would have a diagram depicting products, at a higher energy level than the reactants.Option C indicates a process in which the heat energy of the system does not change, but the diagram itself shows a change in enthalpy.Option D cannot be determined from a mere energy diagram as shown.

questions.text: Energy for the endothermic reaction is given a _ value?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Energy for the endothermic reaction is given a positive value. In an endothermic reaction, the reactants have a lower energy level than the products, and energy is absorbed from the surroundings to drive the reaction. As a result, the change in enthalpy (∆H) for an endothermic reaction is positive, indicating that energy is being taken in or absorbed during the reaction.
Negative: A negative energy value indicates an exothermic reaction, where energy is released.
Zero: A zero energy value would mean no energy change, which is not the case for an endothermic reaction.
Neither positive nor negative: Endothermic reactions have a defined energy change, which is positive.

questions.explanation: Endothermic reactions are described as reactions involving an increase in energy levels of the product. So, as the substrate transitions into a product, the energy level increases, and this addition in energy is denoted by a '+' sign.

questions.text: Which of the following condition is constant in Bomb calorimeter?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In a Bomb calorimeter, the combustion reaction takes place in a strong sealed container (the “bomb”).Since the bomb is rigid and does not expand, the reaction occurs at constant volume .Therefore, it measures the change in internal energy (ΔU), not enthalpy (ΔH)

In a bomb calorimeter, the condition that is kept constant is the volume. A bomb calorimeter is a device used to measure the heat of combustion of a substance by igniting it in a high-pressure vessel (the "bomb") surrounded by water. The heat released during the combustion reaction is transferred to the surrounding water, and the temperature change of the water is measured to determine the heat of combustion.To ensure accurate and precise measurements, the volume of the bomb remains constant throughout the experiment. This is achieved by using a rigid, sealed vessel that does not allow any gas to escape during the combustion process. By keeping the volume constant, any change in temperature observed in the water can be directly attributed to the heat released during the combustion reaction, allowing for accurate heat measurement and subsequent calculations.

In a Bomb calorimeter, the combustion reaction takes place in a strong sealed container (the “bomb”).Since the bomb is rigid and does not expand, the reaction occurs at constant volume .Therefore, it measures the change in internal energy (ΔU), not enthalpy (ΔH).

In a Bomb calorimeter, the combustion reaction takes place in a strong sealed container (the “bomb”).Since the bomb is rigid and does not expand, the reaction occurs at constant volume .Therefore, it measures the change in internal energy (ΔU), not enthalpy (ΔH)

questions.explanation: A bomb calorimeter is a type of 'constant-volume calorimeter' used in measuring the heat of combustion of a particular reaction.

questions.text: Which of this is constant in isochoric process?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Explanation: In an isochoric process, the volume remains constant, and there is no work done on or by the system (as there is no change in volume). The heat added or removed corresponds only to the change in internal energy.

In an isochoric process, also known as an isovolumetric or constant volume process, the volume of the system remains constant throughout the entire process. As a result, the quantity that remains constant in an isochoric process is the volume (V).In an isochoric process, the system does not perform any work on its surroundings or have work done on it. Therefore, the work done (W) during the process is zero. Additionally, since the volume remains constant, there is no change in the volume (ΔV = 0).However, other thermodynamic properties can change in an isochoric process. For example:1. Pressure (P): The pressure can change as heat is added or removed from the system, affecting the temperature.2. Temperature (T): The temperature can change due to the addition or removal of heat, but the change in temperature does not lead to any volume change in an isochoric process.3. Internal Energy (ΔU): The internal energy of the system can change due to the transfer of heat, as no work is done in an isochoric process.In summary, in an isochoric process, the volume (V) remains constant, while other properties such as pressure (P), temperature (T), and internal energy (ΔU) can undergo changes.

Explanation: Entropy is related to heat transfer in a process. For an isochoric process, there is no change in volume, and any change in internal energy is solely due to heat transfer. Entropy change is proportional to heat transfer, and work done being zero in this case doesn't contribute to entropy change.

Explanation: In an isochoric process, the change in internal energy is equal to the heat added or removed from the system. This is because there is no work done (change in volume is zero), so any change in internal energy is solely due to heat transfer.

questions.explanation: In an isochoric process, also known as an isovolumetric or constant volume process, the volume of the system remains constant throughout the entire process. As a result, the quantity that remains constant in an isochoric process is the volume (V).In an isochoric process, the system does not perform any work on its surroundings or have work done on it. Therefore, the work done (W) during the process is zero. Additionally, since the volume remains constant, there is no change in the volume (ΔV = 0).However, other thermodynamic properties can change in an isochoric process. For example:1. Pressure (P): The pressure can change as heat is added or removed from the system, affecting the temperature.2. Temperature (T): The temperature can change due to the addition or removal of heat, but the change in temperature does not lead to any volume change in an isochoric process.3. Internal Energy (ΔU): The internal energy of the system can change due to the transfer of heat, as no work is done in an isochoric process.In summary, in an isochoric process, the volume (V) remains constant, while other properties such as pressure (P), temperature (T), and internal energy (ΔU) can undergo changes.

questions.text: A gas expands from V1 to V2 at pressure P. Work done is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The work done in a process is given by the product of pressure and the change in volume. In this case, the formula "Pressure divided by the change in volume" is the correct representation for the work done in the process.

Explanation: This formula represents the work done in a process where the pressure changes from one value to another while the volume remains constant. However, in the given scenario where the volume changes, this formula is not applicable.

Explanation: This formula does not correctly represent the work done. The correct expression for the work done in this scenario involves the pressure multiplied by the change in volume, not the product of pressure and the ratio of initial and final volumes.

The work done by a gas during expansion or compression depends on the process it undergoes. In this case, the gas expands from volume V1 to volume V2 at a constant pressure P. This type of process is known as an isobaric process.In an isobaric process, the pressure remains constant throughout, and the work done (W) by the gas can be calculated using the formula:W = P * ΔVwhere:W = Work done by the gasP = Constant pressure during the expansion or compression (in this case, P is the given pressure)ΔV = Change in volume of the gas (V2 - V1)Since the pressure remains constant (P), the work done during this isobaric expansion is simply the product of the constant pressure and the change in volume:W = P * (V2 - V1)In an isobaric process, work is done only when the volume changes, and it does not depend on the actual path taken during the expansion. The work done is positive for expansion (V2 > V1) and negative for compression (V2 < V1). If the gas expands and its volume increases (V2 > V1), the work done will be positive, indicating that work is done by the gas on its surroundings. Conversely, if the gas is compressed and its volume decreases (V2 < V1), the work done will be negative, indicating that work is done on the gas by its surroundings.

questions.explanation: Work done = P x (change in volume).

Evaporation is not non-spontaneous as it occurs spontaneously, driven by the increase in entropy and disorder.
This option can't be correct.

questions.explanation: It is a spontaneous process as entropy increases with the change in state. A spontaneous process is a process that occurs without any external energy. Evaporation is a spontaneous process, as it occurs at all temperatures. Hence, B option is the correct option.

questions.text: Steam causes more severe burns than boiling water because it has _.

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because steam only has heat of vaporization. The latent heat of fusion is the amount of heat required to change the state of a substance from a solid to a liquid at a constant temperature. This means that the heat is used to break the intermolecular bonds in the solid, without raising the temperature of the substance. The latent heat of fusion is denoted by the symbol L_f and has units of J/kg.

This option is correct because steam has more warmth and vitality than water. Due to its dormant warmth of vaporization. The latent heat of vaporization is the heat required to change the state of a substance from liquid to gas at a constant temperature. This means that the heat is used to break the intermolecular bonds in the liquid, without raising the temperature of the substance. The latent heat of vaporization is denoted by the symbol Lv and has units of J/kg.

This option is incorrect because steam only has heat of vaporization. The latent heat of sublimation is the heat required to change the state of a substance from solid to gas without any intermediate state at a constant temperature. This means that the heat is used to break the intermolecular bonds in the solid, without raising the temperature of the substance. The latent heat of sublimation is denoted by the symbol Ls and has units of J/kg.
This option is incorrect because steam only has heat of vaporization.

questions.explanation: Correct option is B. This option is correct because steam has more warmth and vitality than water. Due to its dormant warmth of vaporization. The latent heat of vaporization is the heat required to change the state of a substance from liquid to gas at a constant temperature. This means that the heat is used to break the intermolecular bonds in the liquid, without raising the temperature of the substance. The latent heat of vaporization is denoted by the symbol Lv and has units of J/kg.

questions.text: The imaginary surface separating system and surroundings is called _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A buffer is a solution that helps maintain a stable pH level by resisting changes in acidity or alkalinity. It is not directly related to the imaginary surface separating system and surroundings.

A transition zone refers to an area where two different environments or conditions meet and gradually blend into each other. While it can be related to boundaries in some contexts, it doesn’t specifically describe the imaginary surface separating system and surroundings.

In thermodynamics, a boundary is an imaginary surface that separates a system from its surroundings. It defines the limits of the system and is crucial for analyzing energy and matter exchanges between the system and its environment.

An intermediate state refers to a transitional or intermediate condition between two distinct states or stages. While it may be relevant in certain contexts, it does not specifically describe the imaginary surface separating system and surroundings.

questions.text: The First Law of thermodynamics is _.

options.explanation: This is not a First Law of thermodynamics. This option is incorrect.

questions.text: The law of conservation of energy is actually?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The second law of thermodynamics is a fundamental law that states that the total entropy of a closed system always increases over time.
The third law of thermodynamics states that it is impossible to reach a temperature of absolute zero by any finite number of processes.

The law of conservation of energy states that energy is always conserved in a closed system. The first law of thermodynamics is that change in internal energy = heat energy supplied to the system + work done on the system. Hence in both cases, the total energy is conserved.
The Zeroth law of thermodynamics states that if two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.

questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the energy is always conserved in a closed system. The first law of thermodynamics is that change in internal energy = heat energy supplied to the system + work done on the system. Hence in both cases, the total energy is conserved.

questions.text: The heat of combustion is always a _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In a combustion reaction, heat is always given off as the compound burns in excess oxygen to given carbon dioxide. When heat is given off, the surroundings become hotter hence this is an exothermic reaction.

Combustion reactions are often spontaneous under appropriate conditions. However, the spontaneity of a reaction depends on factors such as temperature, pressure, and concentrations of reactants and products. In many practical scenarios, combustion reactions are spontaneous because they involve highly exothermic processes.

However, the spontaneity of a reaction depends on factors such as temperature, pressure, and concentrations of reactants and products. Combustion reactions are generally considered spontaneous under normal conditions. However, it's important to note that the spontaneity of a reaction is context-dependent and can be influenced by external factors. In everyday conditions, combustion reactions are spontaneous due to the strong drive for products to form and release energy.

questions.text: A state function depends upon the _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A state function does not depend on the initial condition of the system only.
A state function does not depend on the final condition of the system only.
As explained above, the state function is independent of the path of the system.

options.text: Both initial and final conditions of the system
options.explanation: It depends on both the initial and final conditions. Hence D is the correct option.

questions.explanation: STATE FUNCTION:A state function is the property of the system that depends on the initial and final states and is independent of the path adopted to bring about the change. For Example, if we heat a sample of water from 0o C to 25o C, the temperature change is equal to the difference between the final and initial temperatures. The way the temperature change is brought about has no effect on the result. ΔT = Tfinal - Tinitial ΔT = 25o C - 0o C ΔT = 25o C OPTION A: A state function does not depend on the initial condition of the system only. OPTION B: A state function does not depend on the final condition of the system only. OPTION C: As explained above, the state function is independent of the path of the system. OPTION D: It depends on both the initial and final conditions. Hence D is the correct option.

questions.text: She _ her lesson for two months next week.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is grammatically incorrect. This sentence is in the future perfect continuous tense, indicating an ongoing action that will be completed in the future. The structure of this tense is "subject + has/have + been + present participle (verb + -ing)."In this case, the subject is "she" and the verb is "learn." Since the sentence states that she will continue learning her lesson for two months, we use the present participle "learning" with the auxiliary verb "has been."Therefore, the correct sentence is "She has been learning her lesson for two months next week."

questions.text: Statement :1: All clocks are watches.2: Some clocks are alarm.Conclusion:I: Some alarm are watches.II: All watches are alarm.

options.explanation: Conclusion I followsConclusion II: All watches are alarm. We know that all clocks are watches, but only some clocks are alarms. This means there are watches that are not alarms. questions.text: Dependent Causes/ Independent Causes:Statement: The government has decided to make all the information related to primary education available to the general public. In the past, the general public did not have access to all these information related to primary education.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: Statement I is the cause and statement II is its effect
options.explanation: This is not a correct option. Statement II states that in the past, the general public did not have access to all the information related to primary education. This sets up the context or background information for the situation.Statement I then states that the government has decided to make all the information related to primary education available to the general public. This decision is a result or effect of the previous lack of access mentioned in Statement II.In other words, the lack of access to information in the past (Statement II) leads to the government's decision to make it available now (Statement I). Therefore, Statement II is the cause, and Statement I is its effect.

options.text: Statement II is the cause and statement I is its effect
options.explanation: Statement II states that in the past, the general public did not have access to all the information related to primary education. This sets up the context or background information for the situation.Statement I then states that the government has decided to make all the information related to primary education available to the general public. This decision is a result or effect of the previous lack of access mentioned in Statement II.In other words, the lack of access to information in the past (Statement II) leads to the government's decision to make it available now (Statement I). Therefore, Statement II is the cause, and Statement I is its effect.
